---
title: "Three Memory Upgrades You Need for Your OpenClaw Agents"
author: "Will James"
date: last-modified
description: "A practical OpenClaw memory stack using lossless-claw, QMD, and ByteRover."
---

::: {.article-banner}
![OpenClaw memory systems](../../assets/images/selecting-custom-memory-systems-for-openclaw-agents/hero-1-v13-logo-three-arms-hero.png)
:::

# This one thing can make or break your OpenClaw agent.

Do you ever notice your OpenClaw agent seems to oddly forget things, drifts off topic, or starts making odd recommendations? If you're using the standard OpenClaw setup you're going to start leaking context and forgetting goals or experience loops of recovery attempts.

You need to give your OpenClaw agent access to better on-demand context and memory. This article will show you how to give your OpenClaw agent the kind of memory boost that is hidden in the best agents in the world.

The setup you will learn now is made of three key upgrades:

- [lossless-claw](https://openclawdir.com/plugins/lossless-claw-a7dqhn) for exact conversation history and auditable recall
- memory with [QMD](https://github.com/tobi/qmd) for local memory-file search and retrieval
- [ByteRover](https://docs.byterover.dev/) for curated durable knowledge

In my OpenClaw deployment, these three parts work together to provide raw history, working memory, and deep learned knowledge instead of forcing one layer to do every job.

![Three-tier OpenClaw memory stack](../../assets/images/selecting-custom-memory-systems-for-openclaw-agents/explainer-1-v14.png)

In the first half of this article I will explain why I'm using each upgrade and how to install it into your OpenClaw. In the second half I will give an overview of recent AI Agent trends that these upgrades fit into.

## Lossless-claw for raw source of truth

First we improve the session logging that is built into OpenClaw using ["lossless-claw"](https://openclawdir.com/plugins/lossless-claw-a7dqhn). This will help the agent keep track of details over a long chat session.

By default, OpenClaw trims session history as a chat session gets longer: older messages are compacted into summaries and the raw detail are lost over time as they are replaced by compacted summaries. The side effect is sometimes the Agent loses track of the conversation as the running memory is trimmed to save tokens.

[lossless-claw](https://openclawdir.com/plugins/lossless-claw-a7dqhn) solves the side effect of compaction by storing every message in SQLite, building layered summaries as a DAG, and keeping pointers back to the underlying detail. The benefit is, the Agent can look up older details when it needs to refresh its memory. This does require saving more log data, however it is not significant in size. In my own setup, the lossless archive has grown to about 2.0 GB after roughly two months of active use, which is a modest amount for preserving a non-destructive raw history layer.

![Default compaction compared with lossless-claw recovery](../../assets/images/selecting-custom-memory-systems-for-openclaw-agents/explainer-2-2026-06-10.png)

### Simple setup instructions for Lossless Claw

[`lossless-claw`](https://openclawdir.com/plugins/lossless-claw-a7dqhn) is available as a plugin and is very easy to install using the plugin installer that comes with OpenClaw. The source repo is also available on [GitHub](https://github.com/martian-engineering/lossless-claw).

```bash
openclaw plugins install @martian-engineering/lossless-claw
openclaw plugins inspect lossless-claw --json
```

Then make sure OpenClaw uses it as the context engine in `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`. Keep its state in `~/.openclaw/lcm.db` and `~/.openclaw/lcm-files/`.

```json
{
  "plugins": {
    "slots": {
      "contextEngine": "lossless-claw"
    },
    "entries": {
      "lossless-claw": {
        "enabled": true
      }
    }
  }
}
```

From there, you can tune `lossless-claw` per agent and per session type. Some agents should stay fully stateful so their history is preserved normally, some can be marked stateless so OpenClaw treats them as temporary working sessions, and some background sessions such as cron or heartbeat can be ignored entirely. If you are not sure which pattern fits each of your agents, ask your OpenClaw agent to recommend a `lossless-claw` layout for your specific agent roster.

My own `openclaw.json` uses a simple split: cron and heartbeat sessions are ignored, subagent sessions are stateless, and ordinary agent sessions remain stateful by default. Stateless sessions can read existing memory but do not save their own turns back into LCM.

```json
{
  "ignoreSessionPatterns": [
    "agent:*:cron:**",
    "agent:*:heartbeat:**"
  ],
  "statelessSessionPatterns": [
    "agent:*:subagent:**"
  ],
  "skipStatelessSessions": true
}
```

## QMD for local memory-file search

In addition to context memory with lossless-claw, we still rely on OpenClaw's built in memory files for longer term memories of preferences, decision making, standards, things it learns through interaction. However we enhance it with [Quantum Memory Diffusion](https://github.com/tobi/qmd), or QMD, to improve searching through these memory files.

Adding QMD to OpenClaw's memory system helps it find and reuse its saved memory faster and more accurately.
Without QMD, OpenClaw would have to search through its memory more blindly, which makes it harder to surface the right saved note at the right time.
Part of QMD includes a search technology called BM25, which basically gives OpenClaw a stronger text-search layer, so it can rank the most relevant saved notes higher instead of just matching words more crudely.

In this stack, QMD complements `lossless-claw` instead of replacing it. `lossless-claw` remains the exact prior-conversation evidence layer, while QMD is the retrieval layer over curated memory files like `MEMORY.md` and `memory/**/*.md`.

### Simple setup instructions for QMD

Install QMD first:

```bash
npm install -g @tobilu/qmd
brew install sqlite
~/.npm-global/bin/qmd --version
```

Then tell OpenClaw to use QMD as the memory backend in `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`:

```json
{
  "memory": {
    "backend": "qmd",
    "qmd": {
      "command": "/Users/superbirdnine/.npm-global/bin/qmd",
      "searchMode": "search",
      "includeDefaultMemory": true
    }
  }
}
```

This configuration keeps QMD agent-scoped by default. `includeDefaultMemory` tells OpenClaw to index each agent's own `MEMORY.md` and `memory/**/*.md`, `searchMode: "search"` keeps normal recall lightweight with BM25/full-text search, and if QMD is unavailable OpenClaw can fall back to its builtin memory engine. QMD's per-agent cache and index can live under `~/.openclaw/agents/<agent>/qmd/`.

## ByteRover for topical memory

Lastly in my memory stack, I use [ByteRover](https://docs.byterover.dev/) to build a knowledge base around the ideas, patterns, and lessons that emerge from all the work I process through OpenClaw agents.

Base OpenClaw gives you conversation history and per-agent memory, but it does not by itself give you a strong historic knowledge layer for cross-session knowledge that has already been judged worth keeping. Without that extra layer, the same lessons have to be rediscovered, rewritten into agent memory, or pulled back out of old chats over and over again.

There are several other efforts aimed at a similar benefit, from Andrej Karpathy's [LLM Wiki gist](https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f) to Garry Tan's [`gbrain`](https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain) and its [launch post on X](https://x.com/garrytan/status/2042497872114090069). They are all reaching toward some version of a knowledge graph or topical knowledge layer that is easy for the model to query over time, while also preserving relationships between ideas so the agent can build deeper awareness around a subject.

### Simple setup instructions for ByteRover

```bash
npm install -g byterover-cli
mkdir -p ~/Knowledge
cd ~/Knowledge
brv --version
brv status --format json
brv locations --format json
```

In my personal install, I keep the ByteRover store outside `.openclaw` in `~/Knowledge`. That keeps durable knowledge separate from OpenClaw runtime state. In this layout, `~/Knowledge/.brv` is the local store, `~/Knowledge/byterover-state` holds checkpoints, logs, and staging batches, and `~/Knowledge/byterover-harvester` holds the harvesting scripts. `brv` treats that knowledge root as a project location, so running it from `~/Knowledge` and checking `brv locations --format json` lets you confirm that this is the initialized tree you are curating into.

```text
~/.openclaw
~/Knowledge
~/Knowledge/.brv
~/Knowledge/byterover-state
~/Knowledge/byterover-harvester
```

If you want OpenClaw-side helper scripts for ByteRover, the setup should stay generic and point only at the knowledge root:

```bash
export BYTEROVER_ROOT=~/Knowledge
chmod +x ~/.openclaw/deployment/scripts/byterover-harvest.sh
chmod +x ~/.openclaw/deployment/scripts/byterover-search.sh
chmod +x ~/.openclaw/deployment/scripts/byterover-query.sh
```

In this stack, I harvest ByteRover from `lossless-claw`, not directly from raw OpenClaw session files. The lossless exporter reads incremental message ranges out of `~/.openclaw/lcm.db`, writes a staged markdown batch plus a manifest under `~/Knowledge/byterover-state/staging/lossless/`, and only then hands that staged file to `brv curate`. The checkpoint at `~/Knowledge/byterover-state/checkpoints/lossless.json` only advances after ByteRover reports a real completion event, which matters because it prevents the harvester from silently skipping messages after a timeout or bad batch.

```bash
mkdir -p ~/Knowledge/byterover-state/checkpoints
mkdir -p ~/Knowledge/byterover-state/logs
mkdir -p ~/Knowledge/byterover-state/staging/lossless
mkdir -p ~/Knowledge/byterover-harvester/scripts
```

Could you do this against raw session files instead? Probably yes, because ByteRover ultimately curates normal files and folders. But that would require a different exporter against session transcripts. I prefer harvesting `lossless-claw` because `lcm.db` is the canonical raw-history layer in this build, so one auditable source of truth feeds the durable knowledge system.

The harvester is run once an hour, its setup is straightforward.

```json
{
  "name": "ByteRover lossless harvest",
  "schedule": {
    "kind": "cron",
    "expr": "0 * * * *",
    "tz": "America/New_York",
    "staggerMs": 300000
  },
  "sessionTarget": "isolated",
  "payload": {
    "kind": "agentTurn",
    "message": "Run /Users/superbirdnine/.openclaw/deployment/scripts/byterover-harvest.sh lossless",
    "timeoutSeconds": 1800
  }
}
```

That cron runs  in an isolated session. The wrapper script keeps the OpenClaw integration thin, the harvester repo owns the export and curate logic, and the checkpoint plus logs under `~/Knowledge/byterover-state/` make the whole flow auditable. In the live setup, the cron also validates the checkpoint and latest harvest log before reporting success, and routes failures through an ops incident path instead of blindly replaying the same broken batch forever.

The storage split is the important part:

- `~/.openclaw/lcm.db` for raw conversation truth
- each agent's `MEMORY.md` and `memory/` for local working memory
- `~/Knowledge` for curated durable knowledge

## Pulling it all together in AGENTS.md

The last thing we do is update our OpenClaw `AGENTS.md` to guide the agent on how to use these different memory systems. That matters because the instruction is short, local, and self-explanatory. It tells the agent which layer to reach for when it needs prior conversation truth versus durable promoted knowledge, instead of leaving that choice implicit.

The snippet should match the real template exactly:

```md
## Memory and Recall

Keep this block uniform across agent workspaces:

- Use the most authoritative source first.
- For current system or project truth, check canonical local files first.
- For prior conversation truth, use `Lossless/LCM` first.
- Use `QMD` for semantic search across local deployment memory files.
- Use `ByteRover` for curated durable knowledge, not exact transcript recall.
- Only answer from recollection or inference when the authoritative sources do not contain the needed detail.
```

That is enough to make the stack operational. `Lossless/LCM` guides the agent toward exact prior-chat truth, `QMD` guides it toward local semantic search over memory files, and `ByteRover` guides it toward reusable knowledge that has already been distilled and kept on purpose.

## Mirroring How the Top Engineers Are Solving AI Memory

Over the last two months, top AI engineers and researchers have advanced AI memory solutions that closely resemble the three-tier architecture proposed in the first half of this essay. They center around similar ideas: layered memory, differentiated tiers of recovery, and selective context reseeding. We can easily see common areas of overlap in the work of Andrej Karpathy and Garry Tan, in [Claude Code](https://www.anthropic.com/product/claude-code), and in other notable solutions.

### Andrej Karpathy's LLM wiki

In early April 2026, Andrej Karpathy published his [`LLM Wiki` gist](https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f) as a pattern for building personal knowledge bases with LLMs. His core complaint was that standard RAG keeps forcing the model to rediscover the same knowledge from scratch. The wiki idea answers that by having the model compile source material into a persistent, interlinked knowledge layer that can be updated and reused. That maps cleanly to the ByteRover durable knowledge tier in this OpenClaw setup.

### Garry Tan's gbrain

Garry Tan's [`gbrain`](https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain), which he also introduced in a [post on X](https://x.com/garrytan/status/2042497872114090069), pushes in a similar direction as Karpathy's wiki by treating a repository of notes and files as a real operational memory surface for agents. That overlaps with the OpenClaw approach because both systems assume memory should be inspectable, editable, and available across sessions. The three tier model differs by separating exact transcript truth from working notes and durable promoted knowledge. The shared theme is that memory becomes more useful when it is organized topically.

![Surreal gbrain memory modules](../../assets/images/selecting-custom-memory-systems-for-openclaw-agents/explainer-3-v2.png)

An interesting comment on Garry Tan's gbrain came from Michael Chomsky on X. In his [April 12, 2026 quote-post](https://x.com/i/status/2043369126631207096), Chomsky wrote that Garry was "kinda correct here, but is oversimplifying memory." His point was that git backed files are a strong interface for storage and inspection, but memory gets hard when the system has to decide what matters now, what is still true, and what should be surfaced without the agent having to think to search for it first. In practice, that pushes memory systems toward ranking and serving the right memories instead of only searching a pile of notes, which is one reason so many serious memory projects start to resemble databases. The OpenClaw three layer setup is trying to answer part of that pressure by separating immediate conversation recovery, working memory, and durable promoted knowledge, but it still runs into the same deeper problem Chomsky highlights: some of the most important memory work happens in more expensive retrieval and search turns, because the system has to actively decide what deserves to come back into context.

### The xMemory paper

Earlier in the year, researchers published a paper called [xMemory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02007). It proposed a more structured retrieval system for agent memory: instead of doing flat top k recall over a long history, xMemory turns the history into a revisable hierarchy of messages, segments, memory components, and higher level groups, then retrieves top down so it can expand toward raw evidence only when more detail is actually needed.

That helps clarify Chomsky's point. xMemory takes seriously the same pressure he was pointing at: memory quality depends on how the system ranks, serves, and reintroduces the right information, not just on whether the notes were saved somewhere sensible in the first place.

In OpenClaw, both `lossless-claw` and ByteRover are trying to impose hierarchy over raw information instead of leaving memory as one flat pile. `lossless-claw` does that through compacting summaries layered over transcript history, while ByteRover does it by classifying and curating information into durable topical knowledge. That is directionally similar to xMemory's effort to organize memory before retrieval, but none of these systems fully solve the hardest challenge here: deciding which information is ideally needed at runtime. What they do share is the goal of lowering retrieval cost and improving enrichment quality by giving the agent more structured ways to find useful memories as it needs them.

## Learning from Claude Code's memory management

The accidental release of Claude Code in March 2026 provided an opportunity to see how a frontier team is thinking about the memory problem. Anthropic's official [memory docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory) and [sub-agents docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents), combined with outside [leak analysis](https://www.deeplearning.ai/the-batch/claude-codes-source-code-leaked-exposing-potential-future-features-kairos-and-autodream), let analysts pull out a fairly clear memory stack: fresh sessions instead of replaying full transcripts, hierarchical `CLAUDE.md` files for stable instructions, a compact `MEMORY.md` index that points to deeper topic files, selective retrieval instead of loading everything at once, separate subagent context windows, and background consolidation passes such as AutoDream, with KAIROS extending that model into long lived daily logs distilled later.

The main themes should feel familiar by now. Claude Code is compacting raw information into organized long term memory. It is building hierarchies over that information rather than leaving it flat. It is selectively bringing information back instead of reloading everything. Each move addresses a different failure mode: context bloat, slow startup, polluted main threads, and the harder runtime problem of deciding what matters now.

## Closing

Taken together, these examples suggest that the OpenClaw three tier architecture is is following a broader industry move toward layered memory systems that preserve truth, restore useful context, and promote durable knowledge without collapsing those jobs into one surface. I hope you find this helpful to your work with OpenClaw or other agents. If you have questions about using OpenClaw you can DM me or leave a comment. Follow me for more media like this. If you have questions about using OpenClaw this way, contact me and I am happy to help.
